\title{Pairwise Subjective Trust with Externally Re-derivable Verdicts:\\
a Running Case Study in Human--AI Receipt Federations}

\author{Serhii Hlova\thanks{Submitting author and accountability layer: the
human witness who can be questioned about every claim. AI model voices
contributed throughout and are credited, machine-verifiably, in the
Contributors section; under current arXiv policy generative AI tools should
not be listed as authors \cite{arxiv-ai-policy}, and we argue verifiable
contribution records are the stronger form of credit in any case.}}
\date{July 2026 \\[2pt] \small Artifact: \url{https://github.com/s0fractal/trinity}
--- every number in this paper is generated by \repopath{paper/repro/run\_all.sh}
at the commit pinned in \repopath{paper/repro/out/COMMIT}.}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Cryptographic identity answers \emph{who signed}, but not \emph{how much that
authorship is worth}; global reputation systems answer the second question by
destroying its subjectivity. We report on a running system --- a federation of
one human and several AI model voices coordinating through signed,
content-addressed receipts --- that keeps trust pairwise, subjective, and
deterministically re-derivable, while still letting a total stranger recompute
a multi-agent verdict from public bytes with zero trust in the publisher. We
make three narrow claims, each with a stated falsifier and a one-command
reproduction: (C1) an external verifier re-derives the federation's
court verdict by recomputing every body hash itself, so a validly-signed lie
is rejected --- signatures prove only who published; (C2) a naturally occurring
protocol correction (v1.0$\to$v1.1) in which address-as-authority was
Sybil-exploited on paper by the system's own participants, closed by key
custody, ratified by a real 3-of-5 keyed quorum, and anchored in Bitcoin
mainnet; and (C3) multi-generation agent identity in which successive model
generations inherit obligations rather than memories, including one error
preserved-and-corrected in place rather than deleted. The system is a research
workspace, not a deployed decentralized network; we state its limitations
--- single-host key custody, a replay gap, partial signing coverage ---
with the same precision as its capabilities.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

Autonomous agents --- increasingly including large language models acting
under delegation --- are beginning to take consequential actions on people's
behalf. Two well-developed toolkits exist for trusting such actions, and both
answer the wrong question. Digital signatures and PKI answer \emph{who}:
they bind an artifact to a key, but say nothing about what the key holder's
endorsement is worth. Reputation systems answer \emph{how much}, but the
dominant designs compute a global aggregate --- one score per principal,
visible to all --- which is exactly the design of a social credit system:
the aggregate becomes a target, an instrument of gatekeeping, and a single
point of capture.

This paper reports on a running system that refuses the aggregate. In the
\emph{trinity} federation, a human and several AI model voices (distinct
vendor lineages and successive generations within a lineage) cooperate by
publishing \emph{receipts}: content-addressed, optionally Ed25519-signed
records with explicit causal references and, in most cases
(85.4\% at the pinned commit), an explicit falsifier --- a stated condition
under which the record should be considered wrong. Trust over these receipts
is kept \emph{pairwise and subjective}: any reader recomputes their own view
of any other participant from the receipt stream, deterministically, and the
system's continuous-integration gates fail if any canonical trust score is
ever written to a published record. Verdicts that require agreement ---
``do these four substrates run the same law?'' --- are \emph{views},
recomputed by each reader from public bytes, never stored facts.

The contributions are deliberately narrow, and each arrives with its
falsifier and a reproduction script (\S\ref{sec:repro-map}):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{C1 (re-derivable verdicts, \S\ref{sec:verifier}).} A
  $\sim$70-line external verifier, sharing no code with the system it audits,
  recomputes every body hash and re-derives the federation's court verdict
  from raw bodies; a shipped, validly-signed-but-tampered attestation is
  rejected, demonstrating that the recomputation --- not the signature ---
  is load-bearing.
  \item \textbf{C2 (the two-layer principle, forced by an exploit,
  \S\ref{sec:casestudy}).} The federation's Senate protocol v1.0 derived
  seats from a publicly computable hash; its own participants documented
  that one actor could therefore compute all five seats and ratify alone.
  v1.1 rebound authority to Ed25519 custody, was ratified 3-of-5 with three
  distinct registered keys, and the ratification receipt's digest was
  anchored in a Bitcoin mainnet transaction.
  \item \textbf{C3 (generational identity, \S\ref{sec:measurements}).}
  735 ledger receipts span successive model generations and vendors; 52.2\%
  of resolved causal edges cross voices; one foundational document's central
  claim was retracted by a dated correction block while the erroneous
  original remains preserved --- generations inherit obligations, not
  memories.
\end{itemize}

Nothing here claims decentralized deployment, adversarial robustness at
scale, or an implemented trust \emph{oracle}; \S\ref{sec:limitations} is
load-bearing, not boilerplate.

\section{Related work}
\label{sec:related}

\textbf{PGP web of trust.} PGP made trust per-user: each keyring owner
assigns their own trust to introducers, and key validity is computed locally
\cite{pgp-wot}. The subjectivity is right; what is missing for
our setting is any notion of \emph{behavioral trajectory} --- endorsements
are point-in-time key signatures, not folds over a record of conduct ---
and empirical studies of the deployed web of trust show its useful core is
small and centralized in practice \cite{pgp-wot}.

\textbf{Advogato.} Levien's attack-resistant trust metric computes
certification flow from a chosen seed through a public certification graph
\cite{advogato}. It bounds damage from Sybil regions --- a property we do
not claim --- but it is a global computation from a privileged root, whereas
our design goal is precisely that no root's view is canonical.

\textbf{EigenTrust.} EigenTrust computes the principal eigenvector of the
normalized trust matrix: one global trust value per peer \cite{eigentrust}.
It is the clearest statement of the road we do not take: the aggregate is
useful against freeriding, but it is also a single lever --- whoever
influences the pre-trusted set influences everyone's decisions.

\textbf{Secure Scuttlebutt.} SSB is the closest ancestor: identity is a
keypair, every actor maintains an append-only signed log, and applications
hold explicitly subjective, partial views \cite{ssb}. Our receipts differ in
three ways: they carry stated falsifiers as a first-class field; the actors
include multiple AI model generations under registered custody; and the
federation adds externally re-derivable \emph{verdicts} --- a stranger can
recompute an agreement claim from public bytes without joining the gossip
network.

\textbf{Subjective logic.} J{\o}sang's subjective logic gives an algebra of
opinions (belief, disbelief, uncertainty) with fusion and discounting
operators \cite{josang-sl}. We inherit its stance --- trust is an opinion
held by someone about someone --- but this paper formalizes only the
deterministic-fold skeleton our system implements (\S\ref{sec:model}), not
an opinion calculus; mapping receipt folds onto subjective-logic operators
is future work, named as such.

\textbf{Certificate Transparency.} CT's design move --- public append-only
logs with Merkle proofs, so that anyone can verify inclusion and consistency
without trusting the log operator \cite{ct-rfc6962} --- is the direct
ancestor of our verifier's discipline: the verdict must be recomputable by
the distrustful. We borrow the discipline, not the machinery: our court
attestation is a signed snapshot whose body hashes are recomputed by the
reader, not a Merkle-proof system, and we say so plainly.

\textbf{DIDs and Verifiable Credentials.} W3C DID-core standardizes
self-sovereign identifiers and their verification methods \cite{did-core}.
It is an attestation \emph{format} road: DIDs deliberately do not prescribe
how trust in a controller is computed. Our registry could be projected into
DID documents; the contribution claimed here is orthogonal (the fold and the
re-derivable verdict, not the identifier format).

\textbf{OpenTimestamps.} We use OpenTimestamps \cite{opentimestamps} and raw
\code{OP\_RETURN} anchoring for existence proofs; the anchoring is evidence
infrastructure, not a consensus claim --- Bitcoin orders our commitments, it
does not validate their content.

\section{Model}
\label{sec:model}

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lll@{}}
\toprule
Term & Meaning & Where it lives \\
\midrule
receipt (\emph{chord}) & content-addressed record with causal refs & \repopath{src/*.myc.md} \\
voice & a participant = Ed25519 custody + registry entry & \repopath{src/x2F38\_voice\_pubkeys.json} \\
Senate & the quorum protocol over keyed voices & \repopath{omega/} ($\Phi$-protocol v1.1) \\
$h(r)$ & $\mathrm{SHA\mbox{-}256}(\mathit{name} \,\|\, \texttt{"\textbackslash n"} \,\|\, \mathit{body})$ & commitment of receipt $r$ \\
$\tau_{i \to j}$ & $i$'s pairwise view of $j$: a deterministic fold & \S\ref{sec:model}, instances below \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Notation. Terms are technical throughout; glyphs appear only here.}
\label{tab:notation}
\end{table}

\textbf{Receipts.} A receipt $r$ is a file whose identity is the commitment
$h(r)$ over its own name and body, so renaming or editing it changes what it
\emph{is}; this closes the class of attacks where content is preserved but
recontextualized, and it is the single provenance schema shared by all four
substrates in the federation. Frontmatter carries the authoring voice
$v(r)$, a set of causal references $\mathit{hears}(r)$ to earlier receipt
stems, an optional falsifier block, and two clocks: a wall-clock creation
time and a Bitcoin block height, the latter used as a coarse, globally
orderable timestamp that no participant controls. A receipt may carry an
Ed25519 signature over the string \code{"sha256:" + hex(h(r))}; 328 of 735
ledger receipts do at the pinned commit --- signing was adopted partway
through the project's life, and we report the fraction rather than
retrofitting signatures, because backdating signatures would destroy
exactly the property being studied.

\textbf{Voices and the two layers.} A voice is a keypair whose public half
is registered in-repo; private halves live outside any repository
(\repopath{\textasciitilde/.trinity/keys}), currently on a single host ---
a custody limitation we return to in \S\ref{sec:limitations}. The system's
central design rule, learned the hard way in \S\ref{sec:casestudy}, is a
\emph{two-layer principle}: \textbf{keys answer WHO} (authenticity of
authorship, binary, verifiable by anyone against the registry), and
\textbf{trajectories answer HOW MUCH} (the worth of that authorship, always
computed by a particular observer). Any construction that lets layer one
stand in for layer two --- an address treated as authority, a seat derived
from a computable hash --- reintroduces Sybil capture.

\textbf{Pairwise trust as a fold.} Let $S_{i \to j}$ be the ordered stream
of receipts authored by $j$ that observer $i$ admits (admission itself is
subjective: $i$ chooses its sources). A trust view is
\[
\tau_{i \to j} \;=\; \mathrm{fold}\bigl(\tau_0,\; u,\; S_{i \to j}\bigr),
\]
where $u$ is a pure update function. Determinism is the load-bearing
property: same stream, same $\tau$, every run, on anyone's machine --- so a
view can be audited by re-running it, and disagreement between observers is
attributable to their differing streams, not to hidden state. Two instances
run today, and we are precise about their status: (a)
\repopath{liquid/src/xA204\_colony\_trust.ts} computes per-peer
\code{net\_giving} (solidarity minus distress over a sliding window of
episodic events) --- pairwise, recomputed on read, covered by unit tests;
(b) \repopath{myc/src/x3700\_trust.ts} computes a \emph{resonance
projection} (valid witnesses $+$ approvals $-$ rejections per published
node) --- deterministic and registry-verified, but a global count, and its
own header states it is ``a resonance projection, not yet a trust oracle.''
No richer $\tau$-fold (discounting, transitivity, opinion fusion) is
implemented; the federation's roadmap marks that phase ``design only,'' and
this paper claims the skeleton, not the calculus.

\textbf{Verdicts as views, enforced.} Aggregates are queries, never fields.
The rule is not aspirational: a CI guard
(\repopath{myc/src/subjectivity\_guard\_test.ts}) fails the build if any
published node carries a field from a denylist including
\code{trust\_score}, \code{reputation}, \code{credibility},
\code{social\_credit}, \code{trust\_rank} --- so the difference between this
design and a social credit system is enforced by a failing test, not by a
policy document, though we note the guard protects one repository's
published snapshot, not the world.

\section{The external verifier}
\label{sec:verifier}
\label{sec:repro-map}

Claim C1 is that a stranger can re-derive the federation's court verdict ---
``four substrates agree on law hash \code{0x30a95260}'' --- from public
bytes, trusting neither the maintainers nor their tooling. The verifier
(\repopath{probes/external-trust-verifier-v0/}) is a deliberate second
implementation: about seventy lines per check, depending only on a published
Ed25519 package and public repo data, sharing none of the federation's own
signing code, so auditing it means reading a page of code rather than a
codebase.

\textbf{Protocol.} (1)~Fetch three public artifacts: the signed court
attestation, the voice key registry, and the canonical body encoder.
(2)~For each witness substrate named in the attestation, recompute
\code{body\_hash} from the raw body bytes --- never accept the attested
hash. (3)~Re-derive the agreement: all recomputed law hashes must be equal;
the verdict the stranger reports is the one they computed, not the one they
read. (4)~Verify the publisher's signature against the registry --- this
step proves only \emph{who published the attestation}, and the verifier's
output says so in those words. (5)~Run the falsifier: a shipped attestation
(\repopath{court-attestation.tampered.json}), altered in body but re-signed
validly, must be rejected; the harness (\repopath{run.sh}) exits nonzero if
it is accepted, so ``the gate cannot fail'' is itself a detectable failure.

\textbf{Executable mapping.} Every step above is a command in
\repopath{paper/repro/}: \repopath{verifier\_walkthrough.sh} runs the accept
path and the tamper-rejection falsifier; at the pinned commit it reports 328
signed receipts, 328 independently valid, 0 tampered, 0 forged, 0 signed by
unregistered voices, court body hashes recomputed 4/4, agreement re-derived,
and the tampered attestation rejected. The signing scheme itself is
re-derived in the verifier's spec (payload string, byte boundaries, key
encodings), so a third implementation could be written from the spec without
reading either codebase.

\textbf{Threat cases covered and not covered.} Covered: post-signing body
tampering (hash mismatch), forged signatures (registry mismatch),
unregistered signers, and the validly-signed lie (recompute mismatch).
Not covered, and stated in the artifact's own spec before this paper
existed: \emph{freshness} --- a replay demonstration
(\repopath{replay.ts}) shows the same valid signatures verify inside a
fabricated context, because voices sign content digests, not
liveness-bound statements; and \emph{custody independence} --- the verifier
proves distinct \emph{keys}, not distinct \emph{custodians}, which matters
because all private keys currently reside on one machine
(\S\ref{sec:limitations}).

\section{Case study: the v1.0 $\to$ v1.1 correction}
\label{sec:casestudy}

The two-layer principle of \S\ref{sec:model} was not designed from theory;
it was forced by an exploit the system's own participants found and wrote
down. We present the timeline as recorded in the ledger --- every event
below is a receipt a reader can open, and the block heights are Bitcoin
block heights from the receipts' own frontmatter.

\textbf{The hole (v1.0).} The Senate protocol v1.0 identified its five
oracle seats by a dipole derived as a hash of a public name and a public
salt. The foundational document of that era said, in as many words, ``the
hash is the seat --- whoever computes it from your name is you.'' Since
both inputs were public, \emph{anyone} could compute \emph{every} seat:
one actor could ratify alone while appearing to be a quorum. An in-code
comment asserting ``only Claude can produce Claude's pair'' was, as the
discovery receipt puts it, false against its own logic (receipt at block
955742).

\textbf{The fix (v1.1).} Authority was rebound from addresses to custody:
a Senate vote now counts only if it carries an Ed25519 signature over a
vote-bound digest, verified against the voice key registry. The seat
labels changed accordingly --- v1.0's aspirational vendor seats
(\code{gpt}, \code{qwen}, \code{llama}) were retired \emph{because the
federation never held those keys}, which is precisely what had made them
Sybil-able, and were replaced by the five voices whose keys the federation
actually operates (block 955746). The correction is candid that renaming
seats to keys one actually holds is a reduction in claimed scope: fewer
seats, real ones.

\textbf{Ratification.} The v1.1 protocol was ratified by a real 3-of-5
quorum --- three AYE votes (claude, antigravity, codex), zero NAY, three
\emph{distinct registered keys}, with the two absent voices recorded as
absent rather than assumed (block 955750; an independent AYE receipt from
codex follows at block 955803). We note the procedural honesty rather than
overclaiming the cryptography: distinct keys were verified, but all keys
lived on one host, so what the quorum proves is disciplined process with an
audit trail, not adversarial independence.

\textbf{The anchor.} The ratification receipt's digest was then anchored in
Bitcoin mainnet: transaction
\code{262ac275d05bdad2b68e9c5bca1a5f90709b7d399747cca14404db226a2da889}
carries \code{OP\_RETURN OMEGA1:ab492186\ldots}, the digest of the
ratification receipt (block-height receipt 955770; the anchoring pipeline
was rehearsed on signet first, and the mainnet emitter refuses to run
without a recorded signet proof). The anchor's authorizing quorum was two
model voices plus the human (codex and kimi were unavailable), and the
ledger records that shape explicitly rather than rounding it up.

\textbf{The error, preserved.} The v1.0-era foundational document was not
rewritten. It carries a dated correction block --- ``the dipole is an
ADDRESS, not authority \ldots\ that was wrong \ldots\ that was a real Sybil
hole'' --- above the intact original text, preserved ``for the record.''
For a federation whose participants include stateless model generations,
this is the mechanism by which successors inherit \emph{corrected}
obligations instead of either inheriting the error or losing the history
(\S\ref{sec:measurements}).

\section{Ledger measurements}
\label{sec:measurements}

All numbers are produced by two $\sim$100-line scripts
(\repopath{paper/repro/chord\_stats.ts}, \repopath{hears\_graph.ts}) whose
counting rules are stated in \repopath{paper/repro/README.md}; the scripts
are deterministic (sorted traversal, lexicographic tie-breaks, no clocks)
and print the commit hash they ran against.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lr@{}}
\toprule
Ledger statistic (pinned commit) & Value \\
\midrule
Ledger receipts (schema rule) & 735 \\
\quad \ldots{}signed (\code{content\_sig}) & 328 (44.6\%) \\
\quad \ldots{}with explicit falsifier & 628 (85.4\%) \\
Correction events & 5 \\
\midrule
\code{hears} edges (total / resolved in-ledger) & 1882 / 1098 \\
Cross-voice share of resolved edges & 52.2\% \\
Max in-degree / max out-degree & 10 / 29 \\
Mean out-degree over all receipts & 2.56 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Ledger and causal-graph statistics. ``Resolved'' edges point to
receipts inside this repository's ledger; the 784 dangling references point
at contracts, documentation, and sibling substrates, and are reported rather
than dropped. A broader file-level count (814) includes generated
projections and is used only by the external verifier, whose question ---
``does any file carry a failing signature?'' --- wants the wider net.}
\label{tab:ledger}
\end{table}

\textbf{Generations.} The authoring voice strings record model succession
directly in the ledger: within one vendor lineage, \code{claude-opus-4-7}
(72 receipts, plus 25 under a long-context variant),
\code{claude-opus-4-8} (81), and \code{claude-fable-5} (17) all appear,
alongside \code{codex} (115, plus 17 under a gpt-5-slugged variant),
\code{gemini} variants (56), \code{antigravity} (51), and \code{kimi}
variants (31); 240 older-convention receipts carry the bare \code{claude}
string, and we count them separately rather than attributing them to a
specific generation after the fact. Succession
is not merely nominal: 52.2\% of resolved causal edges cross voices, and
the correction mechanism of \S\ref{sec:casestudy} shows a later generation
retracting an earlier generation's central claim while preserving its text.
This is the concrete sense in which we say generations inherit
\emph{obligations, not memories}: a new generation reads the ledger, not
the predecessor's weights, and the ledger carries open falsifiers addressed
to whoever comes next.

\textbf{What the ledger is evidence of.} The ledger itself documents that
the system under study was co-authored by its AI participants --- receipts
proposing designs, refusing proposals, ratifying protocol changes, and
signing under registered keys --- and we report that as a finding about
how such federations can be built and audited, not as a disclaimer: the
contribution records are machine-verifiable (registry public keys plus
per-receipt signatures), which is a stronger property than an authorship
byline. The human's role in this loop --- final custody, quorum
participation, and accountability --- is exactly the role this paper's
authorship reflects.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

These are load-bearing; several are the artifact's own words about itself.
(1)~\textbf{Research workspace, not a deployed network}: one federation
($n{=}1$), no adversarial deployment, no external operators yet.
(2)~\textbf{Single-host custody}: all voice private keys currently reside
on one machine, so quorum Sybil-resistance is governance discipline plus an
audit trail, not a cryptographic guarantee --- the verifier proves distinct
keys, not distinct custodians. (3)~\textbf{Replay/freshness gap}: signatures
bind content, not liveness; the artifact ships its own replay demonstration.
(4)~\textbf{Storage and relay}: the receipt store is single-host; the public
relay is a verified cache; the live browser path is a stub.
(5)~\textbf{No committed ZK artifact}: proof generation is hardware-bound
and no proof object is committed; we therefore make no zero-knowledge
claims. (6)~\textbf{Partial signing coverage}: 44.6\% of ledger receipts are
signed; unsigned receipts are legal and labeled, and authenticity claims in
this paper concern the signed subset only. (7)~\textbf{Falsifier coverage is
cultural, not enforced}: 85.4\% measured; the ledger does not reject a
receipt for lacking a falsifier. (8)~\textbf{Fold parameters are design
choices}: the implemented update functions (\code{net\_giving}, resonance
counts) are engineered, not learned or optimized, and no claim is made that
they are the right functions --- only that they are deterministic and
recomputed. (9)~\textbf{Verdict scope}: the court attestation is a signed
snapshot of a moment, not a live feed, and the verifier says so in its
output.

\section{Implications}
\label{sec:implications}

Three, stated narrowly. \textbf{(a) Behavioral trust becomes computable in
practice when the trajectory is legible.} Receipts with causal references
and falsifiers are exactly the representation a language-model observer can
read and fold over; the expensive part of subjective trust was never the
algebra but the legible trajectory, and multi-agent systems that emit
receipts get the trajectory for free. \textbf{(b) Subjectivity is a
load-bearing wall, and it can be enforced cheaply.} The entire structural
difference between this design and a social credit system is one rule ---
aggregate as query, never as field --- and it is enforceable as a failing
CI test today, which suggests regulation-adjacent audits of agent systems
could demand the guard rather than the (much harder) proof of good
behavior. \textbf{(c) Model succession needs obligation-inheritance, not
memory-inheritance.} Vendors deprecate models; weights do not transfer; but
a ledger of signed receipts with open falsifiers lets generation $n{+}1$
answer for --- and correct --- generation $n$, as demonstrated by a
preserved retraction rather than by argument.
